serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of crispy pork strips in Singapore is approximately 120 g (6–8 strips), as commonly portioned at hawker centres and food courts.

nutrient_source.title: Crispy Pork Strips 小酥肉 — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Crispy Pork Strips 小酥肉 (cr_id G00010512). All per-100g values are taken verbatim from the HPB record. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for deep-fried breaded pork (NDB 10219) and documented accordingly. Trans fat is set to -1 as the HPB source does not report it; the input record's claim of 0.1 g/100g trans fat is unsupported by the approved source and is not carried forward.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of protein- and fat-dominant foods — analogous estimate for deep-fried battered pork with negligible carbohydrate
gi_source.source_match_notes: Carbohydrate is reported as 0.0 g/100g in the HPB source. For foods with negligible carbohydrate content, a GI value of 50 is assigned as a conventional placeholder per University of Sydney GI Database methodology for protein- and fat-dominant foods that do not elicit a meaningful glycaemic response. Glycemic load per serving is 0.0 because available carbohydrate is 0 g per 100 g (GL = GI/100 × carbohydrate_g × serving_g/100 = 50/100 × 0 × 1.2 = 0.0). This is consistent with the University of Sydney convention that protein- and fat-dominant foods with negligible carbohydrate are assigned GI ~50 as a neutral reference point, not because they raise blood glucose.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 1050 mg/100g — far exceeds the 600 mg/100g high-concern threshold
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit crispy pork strips due to sodium content of 1050 mg/100g, which can acutely raise blood pressure; if consumed, restrict to a single small portion (≤50 g) and avoid other high-sodium foods in the same meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High fat content (10.5 g/100g) from deep-frying may worsen insulin resistance; starch coating may add unrecorded carbohydrate load
type2_diabetes.guidance: Carbohydrate is reported as 0 g/100g, so direct glycaemic impact is low; however, the high fat content from deep-frying can impair insulin sensitivity over time, and the starch batter coating may contribute a small unrecorded carbohydrate load — individuals with type 2 diabetes should limit portion size to ≤100 g and monitor post-meal glucose.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium (1050 mg/100g), elevated fat from deep-frying, and cholesterol (89 mg/100g) collectively raise cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The combination of very high sodium (1050 mg/100g), total fat (10.5 g/100g including saturated fat ~3.2 g/100g), and cholesterol (89 mg/100g) makes crispy pork strips a high-concern food for cardiovascular disease; individuals with CVD or at elevated cardiovascular risk should avoid regular consumption and consider baked or steamed pork preparations as lower-risk alternatives.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs); pork is rich in arachidonic acid amplifying inflammatory cytokines TNF-alpha and IL-6
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid deep-fried pork strips as deep-frying generates AGEs that worsen joint inflammation and pork's arachidonic acid content amplifies pro-inflammatory pathways; baked or steamed lean pork is a preferable alternative.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Crispy Pork Strips (小酥肉, Xiǎo Sū Ròu) is a popular Chinese dish widely available at hawker centres, food courts, and Chinese restaurants in Singapore. The dish consists of bite-sized pork pieces — typically from the belly or shoulder — that are marinated in a savoury mixture containing soy sauce, sesame, and spices, coated in a light starch or egg-based batter, and deep-fried until golden and crispy. The result is a dish with a crunchy exterior and tender, juicy interior, delivering a rich umami flavour with a slightly salty profile. Per 100 g, the dish provides 171 kcal, 19.1 g protein, 10.5 g fat, and 0 g carbohydrate as reported by the Singapore HPB Foodo database. Sodium content is very high at 1050 mg/100g, making this a high-concern food for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The deep-frying process increases caloric density and generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs), which are relevant for individuals managing rheumatoid arthritis or metabolic conditions. Allergens include peanuts, soybeans, sesame, eggs, and gluten-containing grains. This dish is not suitable for halal diets due to pork content. It is commonly consumed as part of a shared meal at lunch or dinner in the Singaporean Chinese culinary tradition.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the Singapore HPB Foodo database (cr_id G00010512, confidence 0.95), which is the primary approved source for Singapore dishes. The food identity is exact. Saturated fat is estimated from the closest USDA SR Legacy equivalent and documented. All other fields are completed from approved sources or set to -1 with documentation.
notes: Carbohydrate_g is confirmed zero per HPB Foodo database (G00010512); this is a protein- and fat-dominant deep-fried pork dish with no starchy base ingredient reported in the HPB record, though a light starch batter coating is used in preparation — the HPB source reports 0.0 g/100g carbohydrate, which is accepted as confirmed zero from the approved source. Sugar_g is confirmed zero per HPB. Dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per HPB. Saturated_fat_g (3.2 g/100g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for deep-fried breaded pork (NDB 10219) as the HPB entry does not report saturated fat; this is an adapted value and documented accordingly. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, water_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, vitamin panel, and moisture data for this specific HPB Crispy Pork Strips entry are not reported in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16. The input record claimed trans_fat 0.1 g/100g but this is not supported by the HPB approved source and has not been carried forward; trans_fat_g is set to -1. Glycemic index is set to 50 as a conventional numeric placeholder per University of Sydney GI Database methodology for protein- and fat-dominant foods with negligible carbohydrate (0.0 g/100g per HPB); glycemic load per serving is 0.0 as available carbohydrate is 0 g. Allergens confirmed from product description: peanuts, soybeans, sesame, eggs, gluten. Halal status: not halal (contains pork).
